title: Okta — High-volume failed authentication attempts from single IP (possible credential stuffing / brute force)
kql_query:
let window = 15m;
Okta
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(window)
// Normalize common outcome fields used by Okta connector; adjust field names if your tenant schema differs
| where (Result == "FAILURE" or Outcome == "FAILURE" or OutcomeResult == "FAILURE")
| summarize failed_attempts = count(), distinct_users = dcount(UserName) by src_ip = coalesce(ClientIP, SourceIP, src_ip)
| where failed_attempts >= 50
| project TimeGenerated = now(), src_ip, failed_attempts, distinct_users
description: Detects a rapid spike of failed Okta sign-in attempts originating from a single source IP within a short window — a common indicator of credential stuffing or automated brute-force activity that can lead to unauthorized access to protected health information (PHI) such as group health plan data.
tuning_notes: False positives often come from legitimate automated scanners, integration test runs, or corporate vulnerability scans — suppress or whitelist known scanner/service IP ranges.
Exclude known federation / SSO provider IPs and internal monitoring systems by maintaining a managed IP whitelist in the query (e.g., exclude VPN concentrator and datacenter ranges).
Adjust the failed_attempts threshold downward for high-risk accounts (service accounts or admin users) and upward for noisy shared accounts as appropriate.
Confirm and align field names (ClientIP, SourceIP, UserName, Result) with the tenant's Okta-to-Sentinel ingestion schema before deploying; use mapping queries if fields differ.

title: Okta — Successful sign-in following multiple recent failures for same account (suspected account takeover)
kql_query:
// Detect accounts with multiple recent failures followed by a success from a different IP within 60 minutes
let lookback = 1h;
let failure_threshold = 5;
Okta
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(lookback)
| where Result in ("FAILURE", "failure", "failed") or Outcome in ("FAILURE", "failed")
| summarize failures = make_set(pack(TimeGenerated, coalesce(ClientIP, SourceIP, src_ip), UserName)), failure_count = count() by user = coalesce(UserName, UserPrincipalName)
| where failure_count >= failure_threshold
| mv-expand failures
| project user, failure_time = todatetime(failures['TimeGenerated']), failure_ip = todynamic(failures)['1']
| join kind=inner (
    Okta
    | where TimeGenerated >= ago(lookback)
    | where Result in ("SUCCESS", "success") or Outcome in ("SUCCESS", "success")
    | project success_time = TimeGenerated, success_ip = coalesce(ClientIP, SourceIP, src_ip), user_success = coalesce(UserName, UserPrincipalName)
) on $left.user == $right.user_success
| where success_time >= failure_time and success_time - failure_time <= 1h
| where success_ip != failure_ip
| summarize first_failure = min(failure_time), last_success = min(success_time) by user, success_ip, failure_count
| where failure_count >= failure_threshold
| project user, failure_count, first_failure, last_success, success_ip
description: Identifies potential account takeover events where a user account experiences multiple failed authentication attempts followed within a short timeframe by a successful authentication from a different IP address. This pattern often indicates credential stuffing followed by successful compromise of an account containing PHI.

alert_config.threshold: >= 1 account with >= 5 failures followed by success

tuning_notes: Exclude scheduled automated authentication flows (CI/CD, service account processes) by maintaining and referencing a list of known service accounts.
Reduce false positives from geo-distributed legitimate users by correlating with device posture (managed device) or user MFA events — require absent/failed MFA to increase confidence.
If Okta events don't surface client IP consistently, augment with additional fields or ingestion normalization (e.g., parse event.request.ip or network.connection fields).
Consider increasing failure_threshold for highly noisy tenants; conversely lower for privileged accounts and service accounts that should never see interactive failures.
